coredata.dc:title: Resource utilization of sewage sludge as a nitrogen self-doped carbon quantum dot for specific detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Cuncheng
Liu, Jiaxun
Zhong, Xinyu
Liu, Jianguo
Jiang, Xiumin
coredata.dc:description: With the widespread application of sewage biological treatment technology, a large amount of sewage sludge is generated during the sewage treatment process, which not only poses a threat to the environment but also results in wastage of C and N resources. In this study, the nitrogen self-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) with an average particle size of 3.43 nm were synthesized green through a two-stage hydrothermal method. It was found that N-CDs can detect Fe3+ through internal filtration effect (IFE), with a linear detection range of 0–250 μM and a detection limit of 2.207 μM. Furthermore, N-CDs can also serve as a “turn off–on” probe for monitoring Hg2+, with a linear detection range of 2–10 μM and a detection limit of 0.989 μM. Interestingly, oxygen-containing groups were shown to be sensitive to Hg2+, which has been demonstrated for the first time. Moreover, the practical application of N-CDs as a double ion sensor in the detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ has achieved good results for real power plant wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sewage sludge
Carbon dot
Fluorescence detection
Mercury ion
Wastewater

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Open Project Program of the State Key Laboratory of clean and efficient coal-fired power generation and pollution control (D2022FK089) is acknowledged. We also acknowledge the support from the Instrumental Analysis Center in SJTU.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis and application of N-CDs (b) TEM and the size distribution for N-CDs (c)HRTEM of N-CDs (d) XPS spectrum, (e–g) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation (PLE) and PL spectra of the N-CDs aqueous dispersion. (b) PL spectra of the N-CDs aqueous dispersion under different excitation wavelengths. Inset in (b): the normalized PL spectra. Photostability of the N-CDs to the variations of irradiation time(c) (Ex = 365 nm), storage time (d), pH (e) and ionic strength (f) with Ex/Em of 315 nm/425 nm.

(a) The fluorescence response of N-CDs toward various ions (1000 μM). The relationship between the fluorescence quenching ratio F0/F and the concentration of (b) Fe3+ and (c) Hg2+. (d) The PL spectra of N-CDs in the system of Fe3+, Hg2+ and EDTA(500 μM). (e) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs in the system of Fe3+ and Hg2+. (f) UV spectrum of Fe3+, Hg2+ and PL, PLE spectrum of N-CDs.
Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism of N-CDs by Hg2+ and Fe3+.

(a) The quenching ratio F/F0 of N-CDs and o-N-CDs in different metal environments. (b)The atomic proportion of C–O and C = O in N-CDs and o-N-CDs. (c) The quenching mechanism of the effect of the oxidation process on N-CDs.
Analytical results for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection in wastewater using the N-CDs sensor.

head.ce:title: Resource utilization of sewage sludge as a nitrogen self-doped carbon quantum dot for specific detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the widespread application of sewage biological treatment technology, a large amount of sewage sludge is generated during the sewage treatment process, which not only poses a threat to the environment but also results in wastage of C and N resources. In this study, the nitrogen self-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) with an average particle size of 3.43 nm were synthesized green through a two-stage hydrothermal method. It was found that N-CDs can detect Fe3+ through internal filtration effect (IFE), with a linear detection range of 0–250 μM and a detection limit of 2.207 μM. Furthermore, N-CDs can also serve as a “turn off–on” probe for monitoring Hg2+, with a linear detection range of 2–10 μM and a detection limit of 0.989 μM. Interestingly, oxygen-containing groups were shown to be sensitive to Hg2+, which has been demonstrated for the first time. Moreover, the practical application of N-CDs as a double ion sensor in the detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ has achieved good results for real power plant wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The disposal of sewage sludge, a semi-solid material rich in heavy metals and toxic constituents produced during industrial or municipal wastewater treatment, remains a significant global issue due to its high moisture content and poor dewatering properties [1]. It's worth noting that global sewage sludge production has an annual growth rate of around 13 % [2]. The production of sewage sludge in China has exceeded 65 million tons, especially. Currently, direct landfill remains the primary method for sludge treatment, causing significant harm to the environment and wasting valuable resources [3]. Therefore, it is crucial to explore innovative and sustainable technologies for the treatment and disposal of sewage sludge. In addition, it is well known that sewage sludge contains a large amount of organic matter (C), as well as abundant N and P elements, which are ideal precursors for carbon materials. Converting sewage sludge into high-performance carbon materials at low cost and high efficiency is a major challenge for utilizing sludge resources [4–6].

Carbon dots (CDs), a type of carbon-based photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterial, have been the focus of extensive research in recent years [7–10]. Since the accidental discovery of these zero-dimensional nanoparticles in 2004 [7], a variety of carbon dots with distinct sizes, structures, and luminescent properties have been synthesized, including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nano dots (CNDs), and carbon polymer dots (CPDs) [11].

In contrast to other carbon materials, carbon dots possess exceptional optical and electrical properties. They are highly stable, easy to synthesize and functionalize, biocompatible, and have adjustable compositions. These unique properties have led to their broad application in various fields, such as bioluminescent imaging, pharmaceutical delivery, sensing technologies, catalysis, adsorption, and optoelectronic devices [11,12]. The preparation of carbon dots typically involves two methods: top-down and bottom-up. The bottom-up approach comprises various methods such as chemical oxidation, laser ablation, microwave, and hydrothermal techniques [12]. Generally, carbon-rich wastes, organic compounds, and natural products are used as precursors to prepare carbon dots. The carbon sources that have been reported for the preparation of carbon dots include frying oil [13], grass [14], honey [15], saffron [16], cashew nut gum [17], banana juice [18], etc. However, there have been fewer reported studies on the preparation of carbon dots from wastes such as sewage sludge. Most of the existing studies require not only additional drying but also the use of corrosive liquids or toxic reagents. The development of a low-cost and environmentally friendly method for the preparation of carbon dots from sewage sludge is urgent.

Moreover, carbon dots have gained significant attention in chemical and biological sensors due to their exceptional fluorescent properties. Typical metal ions, including Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+, and Cr6+, have been selectively recognized with carbon dot-based fluorescent probes [19–21]. However, most carbon dots are only capable of recognizing one or a few specific metals. Despite numerous attempts to increase their sensitivity, fluorescent probes based on CDs still cannot detect multiple metal ions simultaneously. Additionally, it is necessary to conduct further research to better understand the sensing mechanisms of carbon dots for metal ions. As is well known, deficiencies and excesses of Fe3+ ions have lethal effects on living organisms, and highly toxic Hg2+ can cause a variety of permanent damage to human health even at very low concentrations [22,23]. Due to the widespread application of limestone gypsum wet flue gas desulfurization technology in coal-fired power plants, the wastewater from power plants contains low concentrations of iron and mercury ions that are difficult to precipitate and cause heavy pollution [24–26]. Geeta A. Zalmi [27] prepared CQD using L-tryptophan and tartaric acid for selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. Jie Ren [28] prepared carbon dots using glucose and sodium hydroxide as precursors, and detected Cr3+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ with detection limits of 6.72, 7.26, and 9.51 μ M, respectively. However, the carbon dots of their two groups only support working in a single ion scenario, and it is difficult for the detection system to function when multiple ions are present simultaneously. How to make the carbon dots detect Fe3+ and Hg2+ at the same time has important theoretical significance and practical value.

Based on the above, in this paper, high quality green fluorescent N-CDs (nitrogen self-doped fluorescent carbon dots) were obtained by directly treating sewage sludge taken from a sewage plant by a two-stage hydrothermal method. Compared with the previous methods, this method avoids the additional drying pretreatment process and is environmentally friendly and economical without additional reagents. N-CDs show special fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ and Hg2+, and good effect as a double ion sensor in the detection of power plant wastewater. Furthermore, the “turn off–on” mode of Hg2+ detectors via static quenching and the IFE (internal filtration effect) quenching mechanism of Fe3+ has been elucidated.

Sewage sludge was obtained from a Wuxi sewage treatment plant in Jiangsu, China. The power plant wastewater came from the treated desulfurization waste liquid of a 600 MW coal-fired power plant in China, which was further purified through a 400 nm filter membrane to remove sediment in the laboratory. Hg[NO3]2·H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, CdCl2·5/2H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, MnSO4·H2O, PbCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, LiCl, NaF, EDTA, Na₂HPO₄·12H₂O, NaH₂PO₄ and 20 % peracetic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All experiments were conducted using deionized water. Analytical grade reagents and solvents were used without further purification.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of N-CDs were examined with a Talos F200X G2 (America) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra were collected on an AXIS UltraDLD (Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) and fluorescence lifetime were measured using an EI fls1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs were obtained on an EV-300 spectrophotometer (America). The analysis of wastewater elements is carried out through an inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP) of Avio 500 (Singapore). The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was characterized by D8 ADVANCE Da Vinci. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on the Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer, with a range of 400–4000 cm−1.

Adjust the concentration of sewage sludge to 25 g/L. After stirring evenly, take 20 mL of the sewage sludge mixture and transfer to a 50 ml Teflon-lined autoclave reactor. Place it in a 200 ℃ oven for 24 h of insulation. Allow the reaction system to cool to room temperature, and centrifuge the hydrothermal sewage sludge mixture at 5000 rpm for 10 mins. Then, take the supernatant and perform secondary hydrothermal treatment at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 mins and passed through a 0.1 μm filter to remove the residues, getting a yellow aqueous dispersion of CDs. Further purification of CDs was achieved by dialysis in ultrapure water using a MWCO 1 KDa cellulose membrane for 2 days. Finally, the CD powder was obtained after freeze-drying.

In order to investigate the influence of functional groups on ion detection systems, N-CDs were oxidized to form o-N-CDs (oxidized-N-CDs). Briefly, 50 mg of N-CDs was added to 50 mL of a 5 % mass fraction peroxyacetic acid solution, and incubated at 40 ℃ for 6 h. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 mins and then loaded into dialysis bags against ultra-pure water for 24 h. The o-N-CDs solution was kept at 4 °C until use.

In order to investigate the fluorescence stability of the CDs, HCl and NaOH were used to adjust the pH, and NaCl was used to adjust the salt solution concentration. Due to the effect of low pH on the fluorescence of the CDs, and in order to objectively reflect the quenching situations of the fluorescence by metal ions, all the CDs were configured with the phosphate buffer solution (pH = 6.8) as a solvent, with the concentrations of 50 mg/L. At the same time, the aqueous solutions of common ions were prepared, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ag2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Li+, Mn2+.

The synthesis process for N-CDs is depicted in Fig. 1 (a). The sewage sludge was directly placed into a reactor for carbonization treatment. Subsequently, the supernatant of the reaction solution was subjected to a two-stage hydrothermal reaction, and the green-emitting carbon dots were obtained. N-CDs were formed by polymerization and carbonization of the reactants. The morphology of CDs was analyzed using TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging. As shown in Fig. 1(b), N-CDs have an exceptional spherical shape and good dispersion, whereas the particle size of N-CDs is between 1.5 nm and 6 nm, with an average particle size of 3.43 nm. Obviously, the particle size of N-CDs is not uniform, which can exert crucial impacts on the optical properties. In addition, the HRTEM image in Fig. 1(c) reveals that the N-CDs have a distinct crystal structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (001) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [20]. The scattered peaks appearing in the XRD pattern indicate the successful preparation of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1(a) in the supplementary material, the peak observed at approximately 23° corresponds to the (001) crystal plane of graphite, which is consistent with the HRTEM results.

The structure of the CDs was further characterized by XPS, and the recorded spectra show that the N-CDs mainly contain three elements, C, N and O (284.8, 399.8 and 531.6 eV, respectively) with atomic ratios of 79.53 %, 3.90 % and 16.57 %, respectively (Fig. 1(d)). The high-resolution XPS spectra reveal that the carbon element in the CDs is present in the form of C–C/C = C (284.8 eV), C–O (285.9 eV), and C = O (288.7 eV), whereas the oxygen element exists as C = O (531.7 eV) and C–O (533.1 eV). The high C and O content indicates that the N-CDs have abundant carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface, which provides support for the subsequent exploration of the fluorescence mechanisms of carbon dots. The high-resolution XPS N 1s spectra can be fitted into three peaks at 398.3, 399.4 and 400.6 eV, attributed to pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphitic N, respectively, demonstrating that nitrogen atoms have been self-introduced into the carbon network of CDs (Fig. 1 (f)) [29]. The XPS analysis can confirm by the FTIR results. As depicted in supplementary Fig. S1(b), The peaks of –OH and –NH2 groups are around 3356 and 3233 cm−1, while the stretching vibration of C–H exhibits multiple peaks around 2923, 1494, and 809 cm−1. Aromatic C = C and C = N appear around 1610 cm−1, while the absorption peaks of 1320 cm−1 and 1335 cm−1 correspond to C–N and C–O, respectively. These results demonstrate the success of nitrogen self-doping and the hybrid structure including sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms in the prepared N-CDs.

To evaluate the optimal optical features of the N-CDs, UV–vis and PL analyses were conducted. In Fig. 2 (a), the blue line illustrates the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-CDs aqueous dispersion. The graph shows a broad absorption range of 200–600 nm with a shoulder at approximately 255 nm, which comes from the π-π* transition of C = C in the sp2 core. A peak at 300–450 nm is also observed, which may be attributed to the n-π* transition of C = O on the surface of N-CDs [30]. In order to determine the best optical properties of the N-CDs, we conducted PL and PLE analyses. The results showed that the N-CDs have ideal excitation and emission wavelengths of 315 nm and 425 nm, respectively. Additionally, Fig. 2(b) displays the PL behavior of N-CDs as a normalization function of excitation. As the excitation wavelength increases from 295 nm to 405 nm, the emission peak shifts towards the red end of the spectrum from 430 nm to 500 nm. This phenomenon is due to the presence of various size fractions (ranging from 1.5 nm to 6 nm) in the sample, which results in different energy gaps. These differences in energy gaps lead to significant differences in the performances of photoluminescence [11,31]. The complete ex-em contour map of the carbon quantum dots is shown in the supplementary Fig. S2.

In order to enable N-CDs to be utilized in diverse application scenarios, it is essential to evaluate their fluorescence stability. Therefore, we further investigated the effect of irradiation time, storage time, pH, and ionic strength on fluorescence intensity. In the sensor scenario, it is crucial that the carbon dots have a high tolerance for longtime irradiation. To evaluate this, an experiment was conducted where the N-CDs were continuously irradiated for 120 mins under 365 nm light. The results, illustrated in Fig. 2(c), indicate that the N-CDs remained highly active even after the 120 mins of irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 2(d), N-CDs have been kept in storage for 120 days, and their PL (photoluminescence) intensity was measured at regular intervals. However, no significant change in the PL intensity was observed during this period. Moreover, N-CDs are pH-insensitive, as shown in Fig. 2(e). In the pH range of 2.0–14.0, only a negligible change in fluorescence intensity was noted, indicating that N-CDs can withstand complex and variable acid-base environments. Additionally, N-CDs show excellent resistance to the effects of salt. As seen in Fig. 2(f), the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm remains almost stable even when the concentration of NaCl increases to 1 M.

Selectivity, along with good optical stability, is crucial for developing effective sensors to detect metal ions in aqueous solutions. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs mixed with different metal ion solutions (Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Li+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cu2+, and Pb2+, 1000 μM) were tested (Fig. 3 (a)). The results of the experiment suggest that, with the exception of Fe3+ and Hg2+, the fluorescence quenching of individual ions is not particularly significant. It is worth noting that the addition of Fe3+ and Hg2+ resulted in a decreased fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, with a quenching ratio of 40.0 % and 97.5 %, respectively. To investigate the feasibility of using N-CDs as a fluorescent probe to monitor Fe3+ and Hg2+, we tested the fluorescence emission of N-CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ and Hg2+(λex = 315 nm, λem = 425 nm). As shown in Fig. 3(b), the fluorescence intensity F of the solution gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. It was noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0 /F, F0 is the fluorescence intensity without added metal ions) in the absence and presence of Fe3+ displayed a linear response to the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0–––250 μM. The corresponding linear equation was F0 /F = 0.00639x + 0.976 (R2 = 0.9977). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2.207 μM based on LOD = 3δ/slope [32]. By comparison, the linear detection range of Hg2+ is much smaller, which is 2–––10 μM. The corresponding linear equation was F0 /F = 0.01426x + 1.031(R2 = 0.9955) shown in Fig. 3(c). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.989 μM. Meanwhile, it cannot be ignored that the response of carbon dots with different concentrations to Fe3+ and Hg2+ is also a limiting factor for their application. As shown in Fig. S3 in the supplementary material, as the concentration of N-CDs decreases, the PL intensity also gradually decreases. However, when different concentrations of carbon dots are mixed with the same concentration of Fe3+ and Hg2+(500 μM), the relative proportion of PL intensity attenuation is consistent, indicating that changes in N-CDs concentration cannot affect the detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+.

In addition to the selectivity, fluorescent probe sensors with “turn off–on” functional modes are attractive [33]. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), as a competitive chelating agent, is an ideal fluorescent shielding agent for achieving “turn off–on” mode [34]. Therefore, this paper explores the effect of EDTA on the quenching system of Fe3+ and Hg2+ while maintaining the same concentration of N-CDs as shown in Fig. 3(d). It is evident that the addition of EDTA in the N-CDs-Hg2+ system can effectively restore the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, thus neutralizing the impact of Hg2+. This makes EDTA a suitable compound for developing Hg2+-based fluorescence “turn off-on” sensors. However, EDTA has a limited impact on the N-CDs-Fe3+ system, indicating that these two systems have different quenching mechanisms.

Typically, fluorescence quenching mechanisms include static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), surface energy transfer (SET), Dexter energy transfer (DET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [35]. In order to further elucidate the quenching mechanisms of Hg2+ and Fe3+, fluorescence lifetime experiments and UV absorption experiments were undertaken. Fig. 3(e) shows the fluorescence attenuation curve of N-CDs before and after adding Hg2+ and Fe3+. It was found that the average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs changed from 5.80 ns to 5.85 ns and 5.78 ns after the addition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ respectively, with almost no lifetime decay. Detailed data can be found in Table S1 in the supplementary material. The unchanged fluorescence lifetime suggests that the quenching mechanism is related to static quenching and IFE [36]. Furthermore, Fig. 3(f) reveals that the absorption band of Fe3+ almost completely overlaps with the excitation and emission spectrum of N-CDs, indicating that the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ can be related to IFE. In contrast, the UV absorption spectrum of Hg2+ does not follow this pattern, indicating that it is independent of IFE [33]. While the sensing mechanism for Hg2+ is relatively clear, further experiments and exploration are necessary to determine the mechanism of Fe3+.

As discussed in Fig. 3(d), the shielding effect of EDTA on Fe3+ is negligible, and some researchers believe that the addition of EDTA generates Fe(III)EDTA, which hinders fluorescence recovery [34,37]. In order to eliminate the impact of Fe(III)EDTA, the introduction of fluorine ions (F-) is necessary. Wang believed that F− ions could completely mask the possible interference of free Fe3+ ions on carbon dots [37]. As shown in supplementary Fig. S4, the introduction of F- ions cannot effectively restore the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. This indicates that the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on carbon dots is non-contact. Based on the above discussions, the quenching mechanisms of Hg2+ and Fe3+ are shown in Fig. 4 . In the N-CDs − Hg 2+ system, N-CDs combine with the quencher Hg2+ to form ground state complexes, which weaken fluorescence due to the static quenching. Later, when the competitive chelating agent EDTA is introduced, its strong chelating effect causes Hg2+ to detach from the surface of the carbon dots, leading to a recovery in fluorescence, known as the fluorescence turn-on effect. Unlike Hg2+, due to the overlapping absorption peaks of Fe3+ with the excitation and emission spectra of carbon dots, the Fe3+ present in the solution will absorb the excitation beam, leading to attenuation. Moreover, these ions will also absorb the emitted light of carbon dots, resulting in a fluorescence quenching effect (represented by a purple closed curve around Fe3+ in Fig. 4). In other words, energy is transferred in a non-radiative manner [33]. To be precise, this phenomenon cannot be referred to as fluorescence quenching. Therefore, even if EDTA plunders Fe3+ from around the N-CDs, the fluorescence quenching effect still exists.

Several studies have suggested that the reduction in fluorescence of CDs due to the presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions occur because of the existence of certain oxygen-containing groups (such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl) on the surface of the CDs. These groups have the ability to coordinate with Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions to form complexes [21,30,38–40]. Unfortunately, there is a lack of direct evidence to prove the relationship between Hg2+ and oxygen-containing functional groups in most studies. Therefore, in this study o-N-CDs were prepared using N-CDs as a fluorescence quenching control group. These o-N-CDs contain abundant oxygen-containing functional groups which can be used to explore the sensing mechanisms. As indicated in Fig. 5 (a), the o-N-CDs exhibit significantly enhanced quenching ability towards Hg2+ compared to N-CDs, with an increase in the quenching efficiency from 36.5 % to 80.1 % (500 μM). The XPS spectrum of o-N-CDs is shown in Fig. S5 in the supplementary material, and the comparison of the proportion of oxygen functional groups is summarized in Fig. 5(b). After the oxidation, the proportion of carbonyl groups in N-CDs increased slightly, while the proportion of carboxyl groups increased significantly from 7.41 % to 22.04 %. This increase in oxygen-containing groups may be the main reason for the increased sensitivity to Hg2+. The sensing mechanism of o-N-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 5(c), where the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots act as the primary receptors for Hg2+ in static quenching. The increased content of these groups makes it easier for N-CDs to capture Hg2+, resulting in quenching. It's important to note that the modifications made to the oxygen-containing groups did not affect the responsiveness of N-CDs to Fe3+. This finding reinforces the previous conclusion that the quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ relies on the IFE mechanism rather than static quenching.

In order to check if N-CDs can detect Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real water samples, we used collected desulfurization power plant wastewater as a model water sample. The wastewater sample was analyzed by ICP and found to contain high concentrations of interfering ions such as Ca2+ and Mg2+ (refer to Table S2 in the supplementary material). At the same time, the concentrations of Hg and Fe ions were measured to be 0.60 μM and 0.92 μM, respectively. To confirm the accuracy of the ion detection platform, several standard concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+ were added to the sample. It is important to note that during the detection of Fe3+, EDTA was added to the mixed solution to eliminate the influence of Hg2+. Based on the data summarized in Table 1 , the analysis shows that the recovery for detecting Hg2+ ranges from 89.72 % to 118.73 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 8.32 %. Similarly, the recovery for detecting Fe3+ ranges from 95.05 % to 104.28 %, with an RSD lower than 3.46 %. These results indicate that ion detection methods based on the N-CDs platform have high accuracy, precision, and repeatability. The detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in power plant wastewater monitoring has important potential and application value.

In summary, the two-stage hydrothermal treatment of sewage sludge has been found to be an effective way to produce high-quality fluorescent carbon dots in an eco-friendly manner. This nitrogen self-doped carbon dot has good fluorescence stability and durability, which shows the ability to effectively and selectively detect Fe3+ by IFE (0–––250 μM, LOD = 2.207 μM) and Hg2+ by static quenching (2–––10 μM, LOD = 0.989 μM). Specifically, EDTA can act as a shield to allow the detection system to still perform effectively in detecting Fe3+ even when Fe3+ and Hg2+ coexist. The study also highlights the significance of oxygen-containing groups in the detection sensitivity of mercury ions. The potential of using N-CDs as a “turn-off” fluorescence probe for Hg2+ and an IFE probe for Fe3+ is promising. This work presents an effective and eco-friendly method for the low-carbon and resource utilization application of sewage sludge, which also expands the potential application of waste-based carbon materials in heavy metal wastewater detection.

Cuncheng Ma: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Jiaxun Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xinyu Zhong: Investigation. Jianguo Liu: Investigation. Xiumin Jiang: Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Open Project Program of the State Key Laboratory of clean and efficient coal-fired power generation and pollution control (D2022FK089) is acknowledged. We also acknowledge the support from the Instrumental Analysis Center in SJTU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supporting information about detailed PL and XPS spectra of CDs can be found in the attachment. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuel.2024.132214.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of the application of gasification and combustion technology and waste-to-energy technologies in sewage sludge treatment
Fuel

Review of the application of gasification and combustion technology and waste-to-energy technologies in sewage sludge treatment. Fuel, 316 (2022) 123199. doi: 10.1016/j.fuel.2022.123199.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of the pyrolysis of municipal sludge in N2/CO2 atmosphere
Appl Therm Eng
